story:
Randall sat at the bar of an upscale Boston steakhouse, staring into the amber of his Manhattan, jiggling the pick speared with three cherries. He’d bolted from the firm’s holiday party and sent an urgent message to Tom, an old law school friend who’d come without asking why. Tom nursed a gin and tonic beside him.

“I’m glad you came,” Randall said. “Wish it was under better circumstances.”

He told Tom what he’d seen: her sidling up to Owen, a named partner, leaning in close, laughing at nothing. Tom shook his head. “You’ve got your head all tangled up around this gal.”

Around them, couples settled into corners, coworkers unwound, voices bright with end-of-week relief. Randall felt out of place in the glow. “Not sure how I got caught up in this mess,” he said.

“She sounds like a temptress,” Tom said, matter-of-fact. Randall walked him through the chain—how she’d started with Dwyer, a junior partner, then with him, a senior partner, and now seemed to be making a play for Owen. Tom raised a brow. Was Randall sure anything had happened with Dwyer? Randall wasn’t. Dwyer was a careful man, the kind to protect his career and family. Maybe Randall had been the only one. Maybe she was pushing his buttons, parading herself where Randall would see.

“You’ve got a family,” Tom said. “Let it go.”

Randall tried to nod, but panic ran through him anyway, a cold thread under the whiskey’s warmth. He could see her pressed close to Owen, batting her eyes at his most bland remarks. He waited for the rye to soothe him. It didn’t.

“Lots of people would trade for your life,” Tom went on. “You’re a partner at a downtown firm. You’ve got a coastal house, a beautiful family. Don’t blow that. Call it what it was—a fling—and be done with it. Get a grip. Obsession ruins careers.”

Randall chewed a maraschino cherry and forced a smile. “You’re right,” he said, and wanted to mean it.

Tom drained his glass, slid a fifty onto the bar, and clapped Randall on the back. “That should cover it.” He didn’t look back as he pushed through the double doors and stepped into the cool night.

Randall stayed. He watched the room soften around him in reflections of wood paneling and glass. He thought of his wife and kids and, in the same breath, of her—of the heat, the thrill, the pull that had taken him this far. He cringed at himself. The counsel was simple; the follow-through was not.

Alone with his drink, he turned Tom’s words over. Let it go. Protect what matters. He knew the right choice and the cost of the wrong one. Still, his hand went to his phone. He stared at the screen, at the thread with her name on it, the cursor blinking like a dare. He hovered there, torn between the life he’d built and the distraction that threatened it, feeling the thin line between wanting and undoing. The whiskey sat heavy. The room hummed on. He wondered if he could let go as his thumb edged toward a message he shouldn’t send.
